'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kanbe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}

By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kanbe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田}}."

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kanbe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田}}."

revision.text:
'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kanbe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.

The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.

The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kanbe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田}}."

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kanbe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封}}
<!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)-->
<!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)-->
<!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)-->

Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新|12=draft}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=draft}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|12=draft}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同|12=draft}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=draft}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園|12=draft}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kanbe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=draft}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|12=draft}}."

These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=draft}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.

The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=draft}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|12=draft}}. Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.

Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園|12=draft}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.

Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園|12=draft}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=draft}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|12=draft}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=draft}}
<!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)-->
<!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)-->

{{draft categories| }}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Land systems in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Divine rice fields]] [[Category:神戸 (民戸)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)]] [[ja:神戸 (民戸)]] [[ja:神戸]] [[sn:inko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)}}

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|12=draft}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=draft}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|12=draft}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新|12=draft}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=draft}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|12=draft}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|12=draft}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同|12=draft}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=draft}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園|12=draft}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=draft}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|12=draft}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=draft}}

revision.text:
'''神戸'''（かんべ/じんこ）とは、[[古代]]から[[中世]]の[[日本]]において特定の[[神社]]の[[祭祀]]を維持するために神社に付属した[[戸 (律令制)|民戸]]のこと。[[律令制]]における神社の[[封戸]]である[[神封]]と同義とされるが、神戸の由来は律令制以来に遡るもので、律令制初期においては神戸と神封は区別されていたとする考え方も有力である。
== 概要 == 『[[日本書紀]]』[[崇神天皇]]7年[[11月8日]]（[[紀元前91年]][[12月27日]]）条に初めて神戸・神地を定めたとされており、これが神戸の起源とされている。これを史実とすることは出来ないものの、早い時期から[[ヤマト王権]]や[[豪族]]達によって保障された神社所属の[[部民]]があり、これが神戸の由来であったと考えられている。
[[大化の改新]]以後に元来の部民が神戸として編成され、[[律令法]]における[[神祇令]]において神戸の規定が設けられた。これによれば、神戸より出す租庸調は全て神社の造営や供神料に充てられて残りは税（おおちから）あるいは神税と称して[[義倉]]に準じて貯蔵したが、これを元手に[[出挙]]を行うことを禁止された。[[国司]]が内容を検校した後に[[神祇官]]に報告を行い、神戸からの租税は全て神からの賜物として神社が獲得した。また、国司は神戸の住民の戸籍である「神戸籍」や租庸調の台帳である「神税帳」「神戸庸調帳」などを作成（管理は神祇官が担当）し、神戸の住民の中から[[祝部]]を徴用する義務を負った。その他にも神戸の住民は神社の修造や祭祀に従事する義務を負い、出家得度は禁じられていた（これは宗教的な意味もあるが、主目的は課口の減少防止にあったとされている）。もっとも、当時神社の経済生活は小規模で簡素であり、神戸の住民にそれ以上の賦課がかけられることは無かったから、[[公民]]の負担と比較すれば軽いものであった。なお、神社に対する封戸である神封を神戸と同一と考え、神社に対する部民が律令制のもとにおいて神社に与えられた封戸の民（神封戸）になったとする考えがある。それに対して封戸の田祖の半分は国家に納める義務を有していた（[[賦役令]]封戸条）規定と矛盾しており、神封を例外とする規定が確認されていないことから、租の全額が神社に渡る神戸と半額が渡る神封は律令制初期においては別個に並存していたとする考えも強い。もっともその場合でも[[天平]]11年（[[739年]]）に封戸の租の全額が封主に渡ることになると、神戸の規定との差異が失われ、神封と神戸が混同されるようになったと考えられている。
[[大同 (日本)|大同]]元年（[[806年]]）の[[太政官牒]]（『[[新抄格勅符抄]]』所収）によれば、神戸の総数は170社5884戸であり、最大が[[宇佐神宮]]の1660戸、続いて[[伊勢神宮]]の1130戸、[[大和神宮]]の327戸と続き、100戸以上は8社、100戸未満10戸以上は54社でその他100社余りは10戸未満で1戸もしくは2戸という例も多い。これは[[寺封]]よりも圧倒的に少ない（寺封最大級の[[東大寺]]の5000戸に比べ、宇佐神宮は1/3）が、これは神社が[[寺院]]のような大規模な施設や祭祀を必要としなかったことが大きい。また、[[皇室]]の祖先神である伊勢神宮より宇佐神宮が上回っているのは、奈良時代から平安時代初期にかけての宇佐神宮に対する信仰が盛況であったことと関わりがあるとされている。また、大化の改新後に[[評]]（[[郡]]）そのものが神社に寄進された[[神郡]]における課戸（神戸と同様の役割を果たした）が反映されていないと考えられており、2郡の神郡を有する伊勢神宮の経済規模が神郡を持たない宇佐神宮のそれを下回ると単純に解釈することは出来ない。
平安時代に入ると、律令制の弛緩とともに中央の財政難を理由に神税の一部を神祇官に送って行政・祭祀経費や官人の給与に充てる例も現れた（伊勢神宮の場合、同社と直接関わる[[官司]]である[[斎宮寮]]にも行われた）。だが中期以後、地方政治も乱れ、国司の管理が行われなくなり、逆に神社側が直接管理するようになる。これによって神戸は神社の[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]の一部として編入する動きが進行するようになり、後世における社領の基礎を形成するとともに各地の神社の荘園に「神戸」の[[名 (単位)|名]]を負う荘園が出現するようになった。
== 地名などに用いられている「神戸」 == 神社の土地である「神戸」を由来とする'''[[神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|神戸]]'''（かんべ/ごうど/こうべ/じんご）と呼ばれる地名または姓が今日においても広く用いられている。同様のものに「[[神田 (曖昧さ回避)|神田]]」がある。
== 参考文献 == {{参照方法|date=2023年10月|section=1}} *小島鉦作「神戸」『国史大辞典 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） *水野柳太郎「神封」（『国史大辞典 7』（吉川弘文館、1986年） ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4） *恵良宏「神戸」/時野谷滋「神封」（『平安時代史事典』（角川書店、1994年） ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7） *熊田亮介「神戸」『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4）
== 関連項目 == * [[神郡]] * [[神封]] {{DEFAULTSORT:かんへ}} [[Category:日本の土地制度史]] [[Category:神社]]

The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|12=draft|12=draft}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園|12=draft|12=draft}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=draft|12=draft}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|12=draft|12=draft}}."

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{translated page|ja|神戸 (民戸)|version=100363955|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|qq=}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|ja|大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika Reform|qq=}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingikan|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō Era|ja|天平|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Era|qq=}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Daido Era|ja|大同|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daido Era|qq=}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|ja|宇佐神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|qq=}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Jingū|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Jingū|qq=}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|qq=}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|ja|東大寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdai-ji|qq=}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Shōen|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen|qq=}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (disambiguation)|qq=}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|神戸 (民戸)|version=100363955|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|qq=}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (disambiguation)|qq=}}."

ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマト王権}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Kanda (disambiguation)|JA|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (disambiguation)|qq=}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.

The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). <!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)--><!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)--><!--Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)--> [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)]][[Category:qq]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Kambe]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11589711]] Wikidata name: kanbe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11589711]] Wikidata name: kanbe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=|1=Shinto shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q845945}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|qq=|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kambe]][[ja:神戸 (民戸)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589711]] Wikidata name: kanbe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11589711]] Wikidata name: kanbe [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q845945}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."
== References ==
* Kojima, Shōsaku. "Kanbe." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 3''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983. ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Mizuno, Ryūtarō. "Shinpo." In ''Kokushi Daijiten, Vol. 7''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1986. ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.
== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Shingun|ja|神郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingun|1=Shingun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590937}} * {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}

While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."

ISBN 978-4-642-00507-4. * Era, Hiroshi. "Kanbe." In ''Heian Jidai-shi Jiten''. Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-040-31700-7. * Kumada, Ryōsuke. "Kanbe." In ''Nihonshi Daijiten, Vol. 2''. Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4.

By the [[:en:Datong (disambiguation)|Daido Era]] (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to [[:en:Usa Jingū|Usa Jingū]] (1,660 households), followed by [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingū]] (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."

These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' records that [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reform]], pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]]. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the [[:en:Tenpyō|Tenpyō Era]] reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.

Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of [[:en:Tōdai-ji|Tōdai-ji]]), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ([[:en:Manorialism|Shōen]]), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.

The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingū}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingū}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."
