'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q845945}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kinship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reform}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō Era}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido Era}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingū}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingū}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|WD=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingū|1=Usa Jingū}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingū|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen|1=Manorialism}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."

'''Kanbe''' (神戸) refers to the households affiliated with specific {{ill|Shinto shrines|JA|神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q845945}} to sustain their religious rites and administration in ancient and medieval {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. These households were part of the shrine’s financial system and are synonymous with {{ill|Shinpo|ja|神封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpo|qq=}}, the granted fiefs of Shinto shrines under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}. However, the origin of Kanbe predates the Ritsuryō system, and some scholars suggest that Kanbe and Shinpo were distinct entities during the early Ritsuryō period.
== Overview ==
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' records that {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} established Kanbe and shrine lands on the 8th day of the 11th month in the 7th year of his reign (December 27, 91 BCE). While this account cannot be historically verified, it suggests that shrine-affiliated communities su11orted by the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|大和王権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|ja_comment=|2=ja|3=ヤマト王権|1=Yamato Kingship|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2658252}} and regional clans existed early on.
Following the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika Reform}}, pre-existing community systems were reorganized into Kanbe. Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|lt=Ritsuryō Code|ja|律令法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}}, Kanbe regulations were outlined in the ''Jingiryō'' (Regulations on Shrines). Taxes and labor from Kanbe were used exclusively for shrine construction and offerings, with the surplus stored as shrine revenue. Kanbe-produced tax records, including the ''Kanbe Tax Registry'' and ''Kanbe Yōchō Registry,'' were inspected by provincial governors and reported to the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}. Residents of Kanbe were obligated to repair shrines and participate in religious festivals, while their labor could not be reduced by renouncing the secular world (e.g., ordaining as a monk).
Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|WD=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō Era|1=Tenpyō}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|WD=Q295082|lt=Daido Era|1=Datong (disambiguation)}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|WD=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Shōen|1=Manorialism}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Despite the similarity between Kanbe and Shinpo, the two systems are believed to have coexisted during the early Ritsuryō period. Shinpo required half of its revenue to be remitted to the state, whereas Kanbe’s entire output was allocated to shrines. The difference between Kanbe and Shinpo began to blur after the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō Era|1=Tenpyō}} reforms of 739 CE, which allowed all tax revenues from fiefs to go directly to shrine administrations.
By the {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|qid=Q295082|lt=Daido Era|1=Datong (disambiguation)}} (806 CE), there were 170 shrines with a total of 5,884 Kanbe households recorded in the ''Shinshō Kaku Chokusen'' (新抄格勅符抄). The largest Kanbe belonged to {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|qid=Q715632|lt=Usa Jingū|1=Usa Jingū}} (1,660 households), followed by {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingū|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} (1,130 households) and {{ill|Ōyamato Shrine|ja|大和神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和神社}} (327 households). Kanbe households were significantly fewer than temple households (e.g., the 5,000 households of {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|qid=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}}), reflecting the relatively modest needs of Shinto shrines.
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, central and provincial fiscal challenges led to shrine taxes being diverted to fund shrine governance and local government officials’ salaries. Over time, Kanbe households were absorbed into shrine estates ({{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|qid=Q1550557|lt=Shōen|1=Manorialism}}), forming the basis for later shrine territories and estates.
== Geographical Names and Surnames ==
The term '''Kanbe''' (神戸) persists in modern-day place names and surnames (e.g., {{ill|Kobe (disambiguation)|JA|神戸 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe (disambiguation)|qq=}}), reflecting their historical origins as shrine lands. Similar examples include the name "{{ill|Shinden (divine field)|JA|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinden (divine field)|qq=}}."
